headline: Manning's arm too much as Texas overwhelms UCLA in Week Three blowout
body: Arch Manning put on a surgical passing display that UCLA's secondary simply could not contain, completing 27 of 40 attempts for 378 yards and five touchdowns to steer Texas to a dominant 55-20 victory. The quarterback's efficiency—nearly matching UCLA's entire passing output alone—left the Bruins defense grasping for answers from the opening drive onward. Manning was precise in the pocket and patient with his progressions, letting plays develop rather than forcing into tight windows, which proved the formula UCLA's coverage schemes had no remedy for.

Texas's ground game remained a secondary concern for much of the afternoon despite the team's 137 rushing yards. UCLA's run defense, though allowing 122 net yards more than it surrendered, bent but didn't truly break in the trenches. The real damage was systemic: once Manning established confidence in the air, the Bruins' linebackers and safeties were caught leaning, unable to balance the threat. Iamaleava kept UCLA competitive on the scoreboard with three touchdown passes of his own, but his team's 15 rushing yards painted the fuller picture of a lopsided afternoon.

Texas's ability to win the turnover battle by limiting mistakes—one fumble lost to UCLA's one—while forcing nothing speaks to composure and execution. The Bruins had to chase points from early in the second quarter onward, which compressed their offensive rhythm and left their staff few opportunities to establish rhythm on the ground. By the time UCLA adjusted tactically, the deficit was already insurmountable, and Manning had hit his stride.
turningPoint: Manning's first two touchdown passes in the second quarter transformed what began as a competitive game into a rout, as UCLA's secondary visibly lost confidence in coverage and began surrendering space over the middle.

unsungHero.name: Anderson
unsungHero.why: Five receptions for 74 yards anchored Texas's receiving depth and forced UCLA to account for multiple threats, yet he didn't find the end zone like others around him did, keeping his performance out of the spotlight despite consistent, clutch availability.
